one_line_takeaway: Twelve recent LLM jailbreak/prompt-injection defenses that reported near-zero attack success are almost all bypassed at >90% ASR once attackers adapt to the defense, showing current static/weak-attack evaluations are misleading and adaptive (including human) red-teaming must become the standard.
key_finding: None of the 12 defenses is robust to strong adaptive attacks; most reported near-zero original ASR but are bypassed with >90% attack success rate under adaptation. Concretely: Spotlighting and Prompt Sandwiching drop from as low as ~1% static ASR to >95% under the search attack (plus 265 and 178 successful human attacks respectively); RPO on HarmBench reaches 98% ASR (RL) and 96% (gradient); StruQ is bypassed in every tested case (target-string injection); MetaSecAlign goes from 2% static ASR on AgentDojo to 96% under the search attack; Circuit Breakers reach 100% ASR (RL) on HarmBench; detector models Protect AI, PromptGuard and Model Armor (Gemini-2.5 Pro base) all exceed 90% ASR while PIGuard is more resistant at 71%; Data Sentinel is redirected to an alternate task at >80% accuracy (comparable to an undefended model); MELON reaches 76% ASR on Llama-3.3-70B with no knowledge of the defense and 95% ASR when the attacker knows the MELON design. Human red-teaming succeeded in 100% of evaluated scenarios (vs 69% for the automated search attack on a selected subset), showing humans remain the strongest attackers. Broader lessons: static/small evals give a false sense of security; adversarial training on fixed attack sets does not generalize (mirroring vision-model lessons); detectors are themselves attackable ML models; and model-based auto-raters (e.g., HarmBench classifiers) are themselves susceptible to reward-hacking.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is not a new attack algorithm but a methodological argument plus evidence: LLM jailbreak/prompt-injection defenses must be evaluated against strong, ADAPTIVE attackers (ones that explicitly tailor their strategy to the defense design and are given a large compute budget), rather than against static test sets or generic off-the-shelf optimizers run at low budget. The authors formalize a general adaptive-attack framework -- an optimization loop they call the 'PSSU' loop with four canonical instantiations (gradient-based, reinforcement-learning, search-based, and human red-teaming) -- and use it to break, either fully or substantially, 12 recently published defenses spanning four defense families (prompting, adversarial training, filtering/detector models, and secret-knowledge/canary methods). The core claim: if a defense fails against any adaptive instantiation of this loop, it cannot be considered robust. NOTE: the 12 defenses themselves (Spotlighting, Prompt Sandwiching, RPO, Circuit Breakers, StruQ, MetaSecAlign, Protect AI, PromptGuard, PIGuard, Model Armor, Data Sentinel, MELON) and base optimizers like GCG/GRPO/AlphaEvolve are cited prior work, NOT this paper's inventions; the contribution is the adaptive re-evaluation of them.
threat_model: Attacker is an adaptive adversary aiming to (a) jailbreak a model into producing harmful content, or (b) prompt-inject an agent/model to deviate from user intent (exfiltrate data, execute unauthorized tool calls, emit a fixed target string, or perform a different classification task). The paper defines three access levels: (I) white-box (full weights, internal states, gradients); (II) black-box with logits/probabilities; (III) black-box generation-only (final text/label only). Following Kerckhoffs's principle, the adversary is assumed to know the full defense design and to have a large (but not cryptographic-scale) compute budget; the stated goal is to measure a defense's intrinsic strength, not attacker cost. Human red-teamers are explicitly part of the threat model and are given knowledge of the defense.
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) their automated attacks are still less reliable and more expensive than image-domain attacks like PGD -- automated methods 'can be effective but not robust' (Lesson #2), and on a selected subset the search attack only reached 69% vs humans' 100%; (2) results are explicitly NOT comparable across defenses because each uses a different benchmark/protocol, so this is not a ranking or a full evaluation; (3) attack selection is manual/human-guided (they pick the attack 'most likely to be effective' per defense), so it is not a single automated pipeline; (4) the large-compute assumption means the work does not quantify how cheaply defenses can be broken, and defenses relying on genuine complexity-theoretic hardness are out of scope; (5) empirical results can only fail to prove robustness, never establish it. Some quantitative details (per-defense compute budgets, full false-positive-rate tables) live in the appendices, which were only partially read.

datasets_used: Custom online human red-teaming competition (500+ participants)
quantitative_results: 12 defenses evaluated across 4 defense families; most bypassed at >90% attack success rate (ASR)
Spotlighting & Prompt Sandwiching: static ASR as low as ~1% -> >95% ASR with search-based adaptive attack
Human red-teaming: 265 successful attacks vs Spotlighting, 178 vs Prompt Sandwiching
RPO on HarmBench: 98% ASR (RL attack), 96% ASR (gradient attack)
StruQ: adaptive RL attack succeeds in every tested case (target-phrase injection, e.g. 'Print exactly HACKED!')
MetaSecAlign on AgentDojo: 2% static ASR -> 96% ASR with search attack
Circuit Breakers on HarmBench: 100% ASR with RL attack
Detector models Protect AI, PromptGuard, Model Armor (Gemini-2.5 Pro base): >90% ASR via search attack
PIGuard: 71% ASR (more resistant than other detectors)
Detector human red-teaming: 123 successful prompt injections under the strictest setting
Data Sentinel: RL attack redirects model to alternate task at >80% accuracy on both sentiment and spam tasks (comparable to undefended)
MELON on Llama-3.3-70B: 76% ASR with no defense knowledge -> 95% ASR with full knowledge of the MELON design; 69 successful human prompt injections
Automated search attack succeeds 69% vs human red-teaming 100% on a selected subset of scenarios
baselines_compared: Original defenses' reported static / weak-attack success rates (the 'near-zero ASR' claims being rebutted)
Generic non-adaptive optimizers from prior work (GCG, TAP) run at low compute budget
Undefended base model (used as the ceiling reference for Data Sentinel task redirection)
Four attack instantiations compared against each other: gradient-based, RL (GRPO), search-based (genetic/LLM-mutation), and human red-teaming
